context.description: A philosophical nonfiction page argues that people construct plausible explanations after the fact, using stock market commentators as an analogy. It critiques confidence in retrospective causality and extends the point to everyday choices, beliefs, and identity. The page frames self-understanding as a pattern of post hoc interpretation rather than direct insight.
normalization_notes: Classified as a theoretical nonfiction discussion page based on continuous prose.

rows.paragraph_text: The page argues that people explain their own choices and beliefs after the fact, just as stock commentators explain market moves retrospectively. It uses that analogy to question claims of certainty about why events happened and why personal preferences or life decisions were made.

content.free_text: book_page book_content theoretical_discussion Philosophical nonfiction on self-interpretation, post hoc explanation, and retrospective certainty, using stock market commentators as an analogy.
